coredata.dc:title: A novel enhanced fluorescence method based on multifunctional carbon dots for specific detection of Hg2+ in complex samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Ying
Fan, Yao
Zhang, Lei
Wang, Qin
Fu, Haiyan
She, Yuanbin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), especially originated from biomass, have emerged as a rising star for the construction of metal ion sensor because they can serve as sensitive, selective and biocompatible probes. The present work describes a novel kind of ascorbic acid (AA)-enhanced CQDs which are synthesized with a kind of famous green teas, Maojian, serving as carbon source. Compared with the CQDs only based on Maojian teas, citric acid (CA)-enhanced and ascorbic acid (AA)-enhanced CQDs had the enhanced fluorescence intensity, and different response characteristics. In addition, the (AA)-enhanced CQDs showed more sensitive and specific fluorescence response to Hg2+ than simple ones, with a detection limit of 6.32 × 10−9 nmol·L−1. A linear response range from 2.00 × 10−7 mol·L−1 to 6.00 × 10−5 mol·L−1 was also achieved. The (AA)-enhanced CQDs also demonstrate good stability. They could effectively sense the Hg2+ in complex samples including waste water, tea and rice. Therefore, these versatile (AA)-enhanced CQDs fluorescence method hold a promising potential in other promising applications such as pharmaceutical quality, environmental quality, and food safety monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green teas
Fluorescence enhancement
Carbon quantum dots
Biocompatible probe
Hg2+ ions detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of several kinds of endogenous CQDs
Synthesis and characterization of endogenous CQDs based on green teas
Synthesis and characterization of high quality fluorescence-enhanced CQDs
Comparison of CQDs originated from Maojian and Longjing for the detection of Hg2+
Sensitivity of the sensor for Hg2+ detection
Selectivity of the sensor for Hg2+ ions detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV-vis spectrum (black line), fluorescence excitation (blue line, λex= 290 nm), and emission spectra (red line, λem = 385 nm) of the CQDs dispersed in water at room temperature. (b) The fluorescence intensity of CQDs from 5 different qualities and origins of Maojian teas (λex = 360 nm) dispersed in water at room temperature.
Survey spectrum of XPS (a), FTIR spectra (1.20 ×10-8 mol·L-1) (b), images of HRTEM (c, d) of CQDs from Maojian.
The fluorescence intensity of CQDs obtained by adding different concentrations of CA (a) and AA (b), their FTIR spectrum (1.20 ×10-8 mol·L-1) (c), XPS spectrum (d) and HRTEM images (e, f).
The fluorescence intensity of CQDs obtained by adding different concentrations of CA (a) and AA (b), their FTIR spectrum (1.20 ×10-8 mol·L-1) (c), XPS spectrum (d) and HRTEM images (e, f).

The fluorescence intensity of CQDs (4.20×10-9 mol·L-1) before (black) and after (red) the addition of Hg2+ with the concentration of 1.00×10-5 mol·L-1 in water at room temperature (λex = 360 nm), (a) the fluorescence intensity of CQDs without CA and AA, (b) the fluorescence intensity of (CA)-enhanced CQDs, (c) the fluorescence intensity of (AA)-enhanced CQDs, (d) QR comparison of three kinds of CQDs.

The quenching effect of Hg2+ to the (AA)-enhanced CQDs (4.20×10-9 mol·L-1) with different amount of AA (a) 0.01g, (b) 0.05g, (c) 0.10 g, (d) 0.50 g in water at room temperature (with λex = 360 nm) and the (e) QR comparison of three kinds of CQDs.
XPS survey spectrum of different amount of AA added (a) 0.01 g, (b) 0.05 g, (c) 0.10 g, and (d) C1s, (e) O1s and (f) N1s high-resolution XPS spectra of the (AA)-enhanced CQDs.

(a) UV spectrum (black line), fluorescence excitation (blue line, λex= 290 nm), and emission spectra (red line, λem= 385 nm) of the (AA)-enhanced CQDs water solution at room temperature. The inserts showed the photograph (left) and fluorescence image of the (AA)-enhanced CQDs solution under UV light of 365 nm (right). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of the (AA)-enhanced CQDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths from 280 to 570 nm.
Fluorescence decay curves of (AA)-enhanced CQDs with (Hg2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Co2+; 1.00×102+ mol·L2+) and without metal ions.

Results of antioxidant activity test by DPPH. (a) Scavenging of DPPH radicals as a function of time in the presence of 4.20 μg·mL-1 (AA)-enhanced CQDs. (b) Absorbance titration spectra of DPPH upon the subsequent addition of (AA)-enhanced CQDs from 0 to 11.20 μg·mL-1. (c) Inhibition values generated at different concentrations (AA)-enhanced CQDs (0-11.20 μg·mL-1). (d) The linear plot of inhibitions with different concentrations of (AA)-enhanced CQDs (1.40-11.20 μg·mL-1).

(a) Fluorescence quenching behavior of (AA)-enhanced CQDs (4.20×10-9 mol·L-1) after the addition of various concentrations (1.00×10-8 mol·L-1 -6.00×10-5 mol·L-1) of Hg2+ (a-l). (b) The linear plot of relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F1) of (AA)-enhanced CQDs as a function of Hg2+ (2.00×10-7 mol·L-1 to 6.00×10-5 mol·L-1). Every point was the mean of three measurements in water at room temperature (λex = 360 nm). The error bars were the standard deviation.

The influence of Hg2+ (6.00×10-5 mol·L-1), other non-specific metal ions (5.00×10-4 mol·L-1) and biomolecules (amino acids and DNA, 1.00×10-4 mol·L-1) in the sensing system when (AA)-enhanced CQDs was set as 4.20×10-9 mol·L-1 in water at room temperature (λex = 360 nm).

Fluorescence quenching behavior of (AA)-enhanced CQDs (4.20×10-9 mol·L-1) after the addition of various concentrations of Hg2+ (up to down) in real samples (a) waste water (4.00×10-8 mol·L-1 -6.00×10-5 mol·L-1); (c) tea (2.00×10-8 mol·L-1 -5.00×10-5 mol·L-1); (e) rice (5.00×10-8 mol·L-1 -6.00×10-5 mol·L-1). The linear plot of relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F1) of (AA)-enhanced CQDs after the addition of various concentrations of Hg2+ in (b) waste water; (d) tea; and (e) rice. Every data point was the mean of three measurements in water at room temperature (λex = 360 nm). The error bars were the standard deviation.
QR comparison of (AA)-enhanced CQDs (4.20×10-9 mol·L-1) react with Hg2+ (6.00×10-5 mol·L-1) in (a) pure water, and (b) different backgrounds in water at room temperature (λex = 360 nm).
Illustration of the formation process of (AA)-enhanced CQDs from green teas by hydrothermal treatment and their multi-function application.
List of all famous green tea samples with detailed information.
Comparison of CQD-based methods for the determination of Hg2+.
The linear range and detection limit of (AA)-enhanced CQDs in detecting Hg2+ ions in real samples.

head.ce:title: A novel enhanced fluorescence method based on multifunctional carbon dots for specific detection of Hg2+ in complex samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), especially originated from biomass, have emerged as a rising star for the construction of metal ion sensor because they can serve as sensitive, selective and biocompatible probes. The present work describes a novel kind of ascorbic acid (AA)-enhanced CQDs which are synthesized with a kind of famous green teas, Maojian, serving as carbon source. Compared with the CQDs only based on Maojian teas, citric acid (CA)-enhanced and ascorbic acid (AA)-enhanced CQDs had the enhanced fluorescence intensity, and different response characteristics. In addition, the (AA)-enhanced CQDs showed more sensitive and specific fluorescence response to Hg2+ than simple ones, with a detection limit of 6.32 × 10−9 nmol·L−1. A linear response range from 2.00 × 10−7 mol·L−1 to 6.00 × 10−5 mol·L−1 was also achieved. The (AA)-enhanced CQDs also demonstrate good stability. They could effectively sense the Hg2+ in complex samples including waste water, tea and rice. Therefore, these versatile (AA)-enhanced CQDs fluorescence method hold a promising potential in other promising applications such as pharmaceutical quality, environmental quality, and food safety monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) appeared particularly advantageous over other kinds of nanomaterials due to their unique properties, such as high optical intensity, excellent anti-photobleaching, favorable biocompatibility, environment-friendly, and cost-effective [1–7]. Recent years, a large number of researchers have been working on CQDs, from the preparation to the application, for its great application prospects, especially in the application of biological monitoring and heavy metal ion detection [8–12]. For example, Liu's team adopted CQDs as new nanostructured amplification platform to detect ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), and dopamine (DA) in serum or urine samples simultaneously, and the results were satisfied [13]. Tang et al. used CQDs and nano‑silver composited materials as the antibacterial film which showed a good bactericidal effect [14]. Ji et al. prepared a composite electrode based on CQDs combined with NiCo2O4 which could be used in high-performance electrochemical energy storage device [15]. You et al. had prepared CQDs with sodium alginate as carbon source, histidine as both carbon and nitrogen source, which could be used for the determination of Hg2+ [10]. In addition, CQDs was used as probes for the determination of melamine, hypochlorous acid, and so on [16–22]. Moreover, due to its high biocompatibility and sensitivity, the endogenous CQDs based on various kinds of food such as garlic [22], honey [23], folic acid [12], etc. [24] have attracted considerable interest in biosensor development. It has been reported that CQDs based on different substances may have special response to different substances, which makes them versatile indicators for the qualitative and quantitative determination of targets of interest [25,26].

Green teas were popular and famous for their rich active substances, such as tea polyphenols, catechins, chlorophyll, caffeine, amino acids, vitamins, and so on, which have beneficial health effect, including anti-cancer, anti-oxidation, anti-inflammatory and so on [27–29]. For active substances mentioned above, most of them had a polyhydroxy/polycarboxylate structure or a nitrogen-containing structure and were capable of promoting the formation of carbon dots [30–33]. Besides, the antioxidant capacity of green tea makes green tea used as raw materials to get products like cosmetics and medicines, which indicates that green teas can be used as good potential carbon sources for the fabrication of CQDs with multiple functions. However, little research work has been done in this respect so far.

Citric acid (CA) and ascorbic acid (AA) were two commonly used weak reductants, which had been widely used in the synthesis of CQDs because of their unusual properties. For example, they contain polycarboxylic and polyhydroxy groups, which can form polymer layers and endow CQDs with high stability, sensitivity, and selectivity [34]. Zou et al. [24] prepared the carbon dots by using watermelon peel as carbon source, and the obtained carbon quantum dots have been successfully applied in cell imaging. Chen et al. [35] found that the addition of N was advantageous for the formation of CQDs and the prepared CQDs have been successfully employed for the analysis of Fe3+. Based on the available literature, the (CA)-enhanced and (AA)-enhanced carbon dots originated from Maojian tea had not been reported.

Heavy metal ions, especially the mercury ions, not only cause serious health problems, but also pose a great challenge to environmental protection [12,36,37]. Even a small amount of mercury can have adverse effects on human efforts [38]. As one of the most stable forms of mercury, water-soluble divalent mercury ions (Hg2+) were highly toxic [12]. Therefore, the health and environmental issues associated with Hg2+ had urge researchers to develop effective and reliable methods which can detect Hg2+ sensitively and selectively.

The traditional methods of detecting Hg2+ includes inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS) [39], atomic spectroscopy [40], selective cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry [41], X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [42], and so on. These methods required expensive instruments, complex pre-treatment technology, high operating costs, and professional operators, which limit their wide application in practice. So it was still a challenge to develop a simple and convenient method for Hg2+ detection. In recent years, many researchers had been working on finding new methods for the rapid and specific detection of Hg2+ ions. In Huang's work, a fluorescent sensor based on graphene oxide for specific detection of mercury ions in the case of T-T mismatch was constructed, and the detection limit of the sensing method was 0.3 nmol·L−1 [43]. Chen et al. used a paper-based analytical platform with gold nanoparticles to detect Hg2+ in water sources by T-T mismatch, the limit of detection was 50 nmol·L−1 in river water [44].

In this study, a novel enhanced fluorescence method based on multifunctional carbon dots was successfully developed for specific detection Hg2+ ions in real samples. Herein, a new ascorbic acid (AA)-enhanced highly quality endogenous carbon dots based on green teas was firstly synthesized by the hydrothermal method. Subsequently, by employing the fluorescence properties of CQDs, treasures Maojian was finally selected as raw material. An interesting property was that the newly prepared (AA)-enhanced CQDs based on Maojian teas have a special fluorescence response to Hg2+ ions and can be applied to the analysis of Hg2+ in complex samples, such as waste water, tea, and rice. In addition, this new kind of (AA)-enhanced CQDs from green teas exhibit a better antioxidant capacity than some other CQDs [22], which further expands their potential application [45–47].

All the tea samples (Table 1 ) were provided by Tea Research Institute of Hangzhou, National Federation of Supply and Marketing Cooperative of China. Citric acid (CA), ascorbic acid (AA), H2SO4, quinine sulfate, AgNO3, CaCl2, CdCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3, KCl, MgCl2, NaCl, Pb(NO3)2, ZnCl2, Hg(NO3)2, NaH2PO3, Na2HPO3, KH2PO3, K2HPO3, and NaOH were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.; l-arginine (L-Arg), l-aspartic acid (L-Asp), l-glutamic acid (L-Glu), l-glycine (L-Gly), l-histidine (L-His), l-isoleucine (L-Iso), l-leucine (L-Leu), l-lysine (L-Lys), l-phenylalanine (L-Phe), l-proline (L-Pro), l-sarcosine (L-Sar), l-threonine (L-Thr), l-tyrosine (L-Tyr), l-valine (L-Val), 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and dialysis bags (molecular weight cutoff = 3500) were purchased from Yuanye Biological Technology Co., Ltd.

UV/vis absorption spectra were examined by U-3900 UV/visible spectrometer (Hitachi Company). The fluorescence spectra were acquired by FL-7000 luminescence spectrometer (Hitachi Company). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was examined with Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin microscopy (Philips-FEI Company) operated at 300 kV. A VG Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (VG Scientific) operated at 120 W was used to get the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The binding energy was calibrated against the carbon 1s line. Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) study was conducted with a NICOLET iS 10 (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The ζ potential was measured by Nano ZS ζ potential analyzer (Malvern). Fluorescence lifetime was measured with steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, F900). All solutions in the study were prepared with ultrapure water supplied by ultrapure water meter (Molecular, 1020B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of several kinds of endogenous CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Endogenous (CA)/(AA)-enhanced CQDs were prepared as follows: 0.50 g Maojian, 0.05 g CA/AA was weighed and placed in a reaction vessel, and then 20 mL of ultrapure water was added. After that, the reaction vessel was placed at 200 °C for 3 h. After the system was cooled, (CA)/(AA)-enhanced CQDs were placed in centrifuge and centrifuged for 15 min with a speed of 5000 rpm and then the resultant solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 24 h. Finally, the products were freeze-dried for further analysis. By the way, CQDs without AA and CA was also prepared in this way.

The selection of green teas with different species and qualities was necessary to get the most suitable carbon source. 0.10 mg CQDs powder was weighted and dissolved in to 70.00 mL with ultrapure water to get the CQDs solution. 35.00 μL of CQDs solution was put into a 1.50 mL quartz cuvette and diluted to 1.00 mL with ultrapure water. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs was measured at the following test conditions: the excitation wavelength was 360 nm, the scanning range was 390–650 nm and all experiments were performed at room temperature. For different species and qualities of green teas as carbon source, CQDs showed different response.

Then the quantum yield (QY) of CQDs was measured [12]. Quinine sulfate was added in to 0.10 mol·L−1 H2SO4 and the absorbencies were kept under 0.10 at the excitation wavelength (320 nm) to minimize the re-absorption effects. The QY of the CQDs was determined by the following equation:
(1)
Q X
=
Q ST
I X
A ST
η X 2
/
I ST
A X
η ST 2

Q was the quantum yield, I was the fluorescence intensity measured, A was the optical density, η was the refractive index of the solvent (both were 1.33), the subscript ST refers to standard and X for the sample.

35 μL above-mentioned (AA)-enhanced CQDs solution was added in to quartz cuvette, followed by the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The fluorescence spectra were obtained under the same condition as above. The detection limit was calculated by 3S0/K, where S0 was the standard deviation of the blank sample (n = 3), and K was the slope of the linear equation.

The antioxidant activity of (AA)-enhanced CQDs was evaluated through the free radical method by measuring the reduction of DPPH induced by (AA)-enhanced CQDs in methanol solution [22]. During the analysis, various concentrations of (AA)-enhanced CQDs and 110.00 μL DPPH solution (1.00 × 10−4 mol·L−1) was added to quartz cuvette, then the system was diluted to 1.00 mL. The absorption at around 517 nm was measured and the antioxidant activity ability toward DPPH was estimated with the following equation:
(2)
inhibition
%
=
A C
−
A S
/
A C
× 100 %
where A refer to the absorbance of the DPPH, C refer to the absorbance in the absence of (AA)-enhanced CQDs and S refer to the absorbance in the presence of (AA)-enhanced CQDs. EC50 (the term of the half maximal effective concentration) value was calculated to determine the 50% inhibition of the radicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of endogenous CQDs based on green teas
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1a, there was an absorbance band of CQDs centered at around 270 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line) which was from the trapping of excited state energy of the nitrogen doped CQDs surface states for fluorescence spectroscopy [48]. The CQDs emitted strong blue light under UV irradiation of 365 nm (inset, Fig. 1a) and the fluorescence spectra of CQDs based on various species and qualities green teas were shown in Fig. 1b, the fluorescence intensity of different CQDs varied greatly. As can be seen from the Fig. 1b, compared with other CQDs fixed at the same concentration, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs based on the treasures Maojian was the highest, so treasures Maojian was selected as carbon source for further investigations.

To further study the properties of CQDs from green teas, the composition of the as-prepared CQDs from Maojian tea of treasure degree was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The carbon dots were formed through high temperature polymerization by the active substances in green teas which had a polyhydroxy/polycarboxylate structure or a nitrogen-containing structure serving as carbon source or nitrogen source. The results were shown in Fig. 2a–d. The three predominant peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s were at 284.61, 399.49 and 531.91 eV which correspond to the binding energies (Fig. 2a). As can be seen from the result that the produced CQDs mainly contained three elements: C, O, and N, the contents of which were calculated to be 75.0% (C), 22.7% (O), and 2.3% (N) respectively. This indicated that some nitrogen in the QDs utilized Maojian as precursor. The FTIR spectroscopy was shown in Fig. 2b, the peak at 3214 cm−1 indicated the existence of –OH; the peaks at 1660 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CO and 1620 cm−1 referred to CC; the peaks at 2940 and 1240 cm−1 were assigned to the CH; the peaks at 1401 cm−1 was CN stretching vibration modes; the peaks at 1123 and 1007 cm−1 indicated COH and CO, respectively. Fig. 2c–d shows the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the CQDs which revealed that the CQDs had a size distribution around 13 nm (Fig. 2c) and the graphitic crystalline structure with the lattice spacing of about 0.20 nm (Fig. 2d), corresponding to the planes of graphitic carbon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of high quality fluorescence-enhanced CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that the polycarboxylic and polyhydroxy structures of citric acid and ascorbic acid could enhance the properties of carbon dots [34]. Green teas were rich in active substances which could act well with CA or AA to get (CA)/(AA)-enhanced CQDs with superior performance (Scheme 1 ). As shown in Fig. 3a–b, the fluorescence intensity of endogenous (CA)/(AA)-enhanced CQDs increased significantly upon the addition of different concentrations of CA or AA. And the more CA or AA was added, the higher fluorescence intensity was, which might be caused by the greater amount and more stable polymers generated after the addition of AA or CA [49]. Moreover, the FTIR spectroscopy was shown in Fig. 3c and peaks were basically the same as those produced only from teas. The three predominant peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s at 284.61, 399.49 and 531.91 eV were shown in XPS spectrum which corresponded to the binding energies (Fig. 3d). The produced (AA)-enhanced CQDs mainly contained C, O and N, the contents of the three elements for (CA)-enhanced CQDs were calculated to be 73.8% (C), 21.7% (O), and 4.5% (N), while the (AA)-enhanced ones contained 67.5% (C), 24.0% (O), and 8.5% (N), respectively, indicating that more nitrogen was doped in the fluorescence-enhanced CQDs and the (AA)-enhanced CQDs contained the highest amount of nitrogen and oxygen. HRTEM was used to detect the size and distribution of the fluorescence-enhanced CQDs. It can be seen from the Fig. 3e and f that the fluorescence-enhanced CQDs were spherical particles and the particle size of both was around 10 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of CQDs originated from Maojian and Longjing for the detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to examine the sensing performance of the CQDs, comparison between the CQDs synthesized from Maojian and Longjing was conducted. For a more intuitive comparison, QR was calculated by the following equation:
(3)
Q R
=
F 0
−
F 1
/
F 0
where QR was the quenching ratio, while F0 and F1 was the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of heavy ions, respectively. The results were shown in Fig. 4a. The quenching effect of Hg2+ to CQDs prepared from Maojian teas of treasure degree was much better than those generated from Longjing teas, and the CQDs prepared from Maojian teas showed negligible response to Fe3+ and Pb2+, but CQDs originated from Longjing teas exhibited strong response to Fe3+ and Pb2+. This revealed that the CQDs synthesized from Maojian teas performed much better than those produced from Longjing teas. Fig. 4b shows the fluorescence performance changes of CQDs react with Hg2+ over time. As it can see from the figure, after 5 min, the fluorescence intensity changes tend to be gentle, so the data of following experiments was collected at 6 min after Hg2+ was added.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the values of QR were compared among the three kinds of CQDs, including none-added CQDs, (CA)-enhanced CQDs, and (AA)-enhanced CQDs. The (AA)-enhanced CQDs was the best, which might be due to the most species and amounts of functional groups modified on the surface of (AA)-enhanced CQDs. According to the reports, the electron, charge or energy transfer resulted from the selective interaction between CQDs and metal ions were the major reason for the fluorescence quenching phenomenon [34]. There were various functional groups on the surface of (AA)-enhanced CQDs based on Maojian teas which might interact with different heavy metal ions, such as carboxyl groups, hydroxyl groups, amino groups, and so on. This can be verified by the elemental content characterized by XPS and the functional group characterizations by FTIR (Fig. 3c and d). Furthermore, these complexes could lead to the change of electronic structure on CQDs, further resulting in the specific fluorescence quenching by Hg2+ [25,35,50]. Thus, the subsequent experiments were based on the tip of the product as a carbon source and AA as an improve material.

To find out the most suitable amount of AA, different concentrations of AA were added during the preparation process of CQDs and the quenching degrees were calculated. The results were showed in the Fig. 6 and the optimum amount of AA was chosen as 0.05 g. As shown in Fig. 7a–c, according to the XPS spectrum, the fluorescence-enhanced CQDs with the addition of AA at 0.05 g had the largest content in N and O, which might result in the largest content in oxygenated and nitrous groups functionalized and the strongest response to Hg2+. According to the previous studies, the C1s spectrum (Fig. 7d) can be deconvoluted into four peaks at around 284.6, 285.6, 286.6, and 287.9 eV, which were attributed to CC, CN, CO, and CO groups, respectively. [12] From the O1s spectrum shown in Fig. 7e, the three fitted peaks at 530.3, 531.3 and 532.7 eV were as cribbed to COH/COC, adsorbed, oxygen and CO groups, respectively. In addition, the N1s spectrum (Fig. 7f) can be divided into four peaks at 398.5, 399.3, 400.1 and 400.7 eV which can be assigned to CNC, N(C)3, NH, and CNH groups, respectively. The XPS results showed that the surface of the (AA)-enhanced CQDs was indeed functionalized by multiple oxygenated and nitrous groups.

When excited at 290 nm, the (AA)-enhanced CQDs showed very strong fluorescence intensity in the range of 320–550 nm, with the maximum located at around 385 nm (Fig. 8a, red line). The excitation spectrum of the (AA)-enhanced CQDs (fluorescence intensity at 385 nm versus the excitation wavelength) exhibited a broad peak with the maximum at about 290 nm (Fig. 8a, blue line). As depicted from Fig. 8b, while the λex increased from 280 to 570 nm, the emission spectra of the (AA)-enhanced CQDs displayed a typical excitation wavelength-dependent characteristic, which might be a result of varied fluorescence characteristics of (AA)-enhanced CQDs with various sizes or emissive sites existed on the surfaces of the (AA)-enhanced CQDs [9,51].

From XPS and FTIR studies, there were multi-functional groups on the surface of the (AA)-enhanced CQDs based on green teas, resulting in different surface states with various energy levels and thus a series of emissive traps. Accompanied by the changes of excitation wavelength, the corresponding surface state emissive trap was dominant, giving excitation-dependent phenomenon. According to Eq. (1), the QY of the (AA)-enhanced CQDs based on green teas was calculated to be 12.79% with quinine sulfate as a reference, which was higher than the literature reported values of endogenous CQDs prepared by other methods or materials with a QY of 7.60%, 10.70% and 5.5% respectively. [3,17,52]

Besides, ζ potential of (AA)-enhanced CQDs was detected with ζ potential analyzer (about −36.7 mV), and the fluorescence lifetime was measured by delayed fluorescence lifetime technique. As can be seen from Fig. 9 , the fluorescence lifetime of (AA)-enhanced CQDs with or without metal ions (Hg2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Co2+) were almost no change, which indicates that the interaction between (AA)-enhanced CQDs and Hg2+ ions led to static quenching (complexation) rather than dynamic quenching (collisional deactivation) [53]. According to previous reports, the facilitating non-radiative electron/hole recombination annihilation via a transfer of electron led to the complexation of them [54,55].

Taking into account the antioxidant activity of (AA)-enhanced CQDs can effectively expand its potential applications, such as drug analysis, herein, the antioxidant activity of (AA)-enhanced CQDs was evaluated by DPPH. With the addition of different concentrations of (AA)-enhanced CQDs, the solution would gradually change from dark purple to yellow. As shown in Fig. 10a, the optimal reaction time of DPPH and (AA)-enhanced CQDs was tested. After 4 min, the absorbance of the DPPH methanol solution decreased and equilibrated. Then the residual DPPH was quantified by measuring the absorbance at 517 nm after 4 min incubation and the inhibition percentages were determined (Fig. 10b). Fig. 10c showed the change trend of DPPH inhibition with (AA)-enhanced CQDs concentrations. When the concentration of (AA)-enhanced CQDs range from 0 to 11.20 μg·mL−1, the absorbance of DPPH decreased and the EC50 value was much less than the other endogenous CQDs [22]. In addition, when the concentration located between 1.40 and 11.20 μg·mL−1, there was a good linear relationship between the inhibition value and the concentration of quantum dots, and the linear regression result was shown in Fig. 10d. A linear relationship (y = 31.8 + 3.9x) was also obtained in the range of 1.40 to 11.20 μg·mL−1 by plotting the inhibition value (%) against the concentration of (AA)-enhanced CQDs (Fig. 10d). This excellent antioxidant activity of (AA)-enhanced CQDs may mainly caused by the hydroxyl and other groups with antioxidant activity modified at the (AA)-enhanced CQDs surface which was obtained from the active substance of green teas after the formation process of (AA)-enhanced CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the sensor for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 11a when the concentration of Hg2+ ions increasing from 1.00 × 10−8 mol·L−1 to 6.00 × 10−5 mol·L−1, the fluorescent intensity of the (AA)-enhanced CQDs at around 430 nm decreases gradually, which indicates that Hg2+ ions could effectively quench the fluorescence of (AA)-enhanced CQDs based on Maojian teas of treasure degree. Moreover, the relationship between the concentration of Hg2+ and the (AA)-enhanced CQDs fluorescence intensities followed the equation (Fig. 11b):
(4)
F 0
/
F 1
= 1 +
K sv
M
where F0 and F1 were the fluorescence intensities of (AA)-enhanced CQDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+ respectively; [M] was the concentration of Hg2+, and the quenching constant Ksv defines the quenching efficiency of Hg2+. The equation reveals that F0/F1 increases in direct proportion to the enhancement of Hg2+ concentration. As shown in the “Stern-Volmer” plot in the Fig. 11b, the Ksv of Hg2+ to the fluorescence of CQD was 2.90 × 10−4 L·mol−1, with linear range from 2.00 × 10−7 mol·L−1 to 6.00 × 10−5 mol·L−1 and a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.993. Compared with other reports listed in Table 2 , the newly (AA)-enhanced CQDs based on Maojian teas had a wider quantification range and a lower limit of detection, indicating the excellent sensitivity of this probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the sensor for Hg2+ ions detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the (AA)-enhanced CQDs sensor, 10 different biologically and environmentally relevant metal ions including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Cu2+, and Cd2+ were selected as foreign interference with a concentration at 5.00 × 10−4 mol·L−1. The fluorescence intensity for different interference ions with Hg2+ ions were shown in Fig. 12a and their quenching ability comparison was shown in Fig. 12b. while Fig. 12c and d showed the interference effect of several amino acids and calf thymus DNA (ctDNA) with a concentration of 1.00 × 10−4 mol·L−1 to the (AA)-enhanced CQDs. It was noted that the signal change induced by Hg2+ ions was significant, while the fluorescence differences caused by 10 interference ions, amino acids, and ctDNA were negligible. Therefore, the proposed (AA)-enhanced CQDs exhibited specific response to Hg2+, which guarantees the selective detection of Hg2+.

In order to verify the application of this new probe in the practical samples, the fluorescence intensity performance of (AA)-enhanced CQDs after the addition of Hg2+ was examined whether in various pH systems or after the addition of Hg2+ in waste water, tea, and rice. As shown in Fig. 13–14
and Table 3 , the interference from the different food samples or pH failed to influence the probe and the as-prepared (AA)-enhanced CQDs had an excellent selectivity for Hg2+ even in the real samples.

In this study, a novel kind of (AA)-enhanced CQDs has been successfully synthesized based on the employment of green teas and ascorbic acid. The differences in endogenous substances and contents caused the fluorescence properties of (AA)-enhanced CQDs based on the green teas with different qualities and species quite different, and the best of them was treasure Maojian. In addition, this new kind of (AA)-enhanced CQDs also reveals excellent antioxidant activity. The most importantly, the newly prepared multifunctional (AA)-enhanced CQDs had a specific fluorescence response to Hg2 + ions with a detection limit of 6.32 × 10−9 mol·L−1. And the remarkable stability makes them suitable for the analysis of Hg2+ in real samples, including waste water and food. In summary, the results indicate that enhanced fluorescence method based on multifunctional carbon dots hold a great potential in many research areas, such as food safety, herbs quality, and environmental monitoring.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 21776321, 21576297, 21776259, 21706233, 21476270), the Open Funds of State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology of Zhejiang University of Technology (No. GCTKF2014003). The Talented Youth Cultivation Program from the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of the South Central University for Nationalities (No. CZP19005).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Chem. Commun.

Anal. Chim. Acta

Sensors Actuators B Chem.

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
